description:
You are an integration developer who wants a **minimal-yet-reusable shell automation (5–10 commands long)** that summarizes two sample API responses that have been placed on disk for offline testing.

Existing resources (already present in the workspace):
1. /home/user/mock_responses/status.json  
   └── Contains a single-line JSON string: {"service":"alpha","status":"ok"}
2. /home/user/mock_responses/metrics.json  
   └── Contains a single-line JSON string: {"requests":123,"errors":0,"uptime":"48h"}

The assignment – all actions must be performed from the regular user account without sudo:

A. Create the directory /home/user/scripts if it does not yet exist.  
B. Inside that directory write a shell script named api_summary.sh that fulfils all the following technical requirements:  
   1. File must start with the standard bash shebang (#!/usr/bin/env bash).  
   2. Total length is strictly between **5 and 10 non-blank, non-comment commands** (variable assignments and pipes count as commands).  
   3. The script must:  
      a. Read /home/user/mock_responses/status.json, extract the value of the keys “service” and “status”.  
      b. Read /home/user/mock_responses/metrics.json, extract the integer values of “requests” and “errors”.  
      c. Obtain the current UTC time stamp formatted exactly as: YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ (example: 2025-01-07T14:05:17Z).  
      d. Compose a single pipe-separated summary line in the exact order shown below and **append** it to /home/user/logs/api_summary.log (create directories/files as needed).  
         Required line layout (no spaces anywhere except inside the timestamp):  
         <timestamp>|service:<service>|status:<status>|requests:<requests>|errors:<errors>  
         Example valid line: 2025-01-07T14:05:17Z|service:alpha|status:ok|requests:123|errors:0  
   4. Do not rely on external JSON-specific tools that might be absent (e.g., jq). Use standard POSIX text utilities (grep, sed, awk, cut, etc.).  

C. Ensure both /home/user/scripts/api_summary.sh and /home/user/logs/api_summary.log end up with 644 permissions, and the script has the executable bit set (755 is fine).

D. For easier verification, append (do **not** overwrite) exactly one new log entry by running api_summary.sh once after creation.

E. Output nothing except normal command-line diagnostics produced while you work; all verification by the grader will look at the filesystem, not your stdout.

Automated test checks:
1. /home/user/scripts/api_summary.sh exists, is executable, and contains 5–10 commands.  
2. /home/user/logs/api_summary.log exists and its **last** line matches the regex:  
   ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z\|service:alpha\|status:ok\|requests:123\|errors:0$  
3. The script must be idempotent: every subsequent run appends exactly one new correctly-formatted line.  

Complete the task so that the above automated checks will pass.
truth:
Initial files present before the agent starts:

1. Path: /home/user/mock_responses/status.json  
   Content (single line, no trailing newline):  
   {"service":"alpha","status":"ok"}

2. Path: /home/user/mock_responses/metrics.json  
   Content (single line, no trailing newline):  
   {"requests":123,"errors":0,"uptime":"48h"}

Additional verification details used by the grader:

• The grader will inspect /home/user/scripts/api_summary.sh and count the number of non-blank, non-comment lines after the shebang; it must be >=5 and <=10.  
• The log file inspected is /home/user/logs/api_summary.log. If multiple lines exist, only the last will be matched against the regex specified in the public task.  
• Permissions the grader expects:  
  – api_summary.sh : -rwxr-xr-x (755) or -rwxr--r-- (744) acceptable, but execute bit must be on for owner.  
  – api_summary.log : -rw-r--r-- (644).  
• The grader will execute the script once after its own checks to ensure it appends a new line following the required pattern, proving idempotency.

No other hidden constraints.
